mnemonics.title: Code Rate Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember: Code Rate = k/n = Keep over n (total). Higher rate = less redundancy = less protection.

mnemonics.title: Error Detection/Correction Formula
mnemonics.content: Detect: d_min - 1; Correct: floor((d_min - 1)/2). Think: Detect MORE errors than you correct!

mnemonics.title: Hamming Code Parity Positions
mnemonics.content: Parity bits at positions 1,2,4,8... (powers of 2). Each parity bit covers positions with that bit set in binary.

acronyms.usage: Error detection in data links, Ethernet, ZIP files
Simple error detection in memory systems
Binary field for algebraic operations in coding theory

acronyms.expansion: Single Error Correction Double Error Detection
acronyms.usage: Memory error correction in servers and critical systems

visualTips.visualization: Draw 7-bit codeword with positions marked 1-7. Show parity bits at 1,2,4 with data bits at 3,5,6,7. Draw circles showing which parity covers which bits.
Create a 4x4 grid. Fill with data bits. Add parity column on right (row parity) and parity row at bottom (column parity). Show how single error at intersection identifies both row and column.
Show syndrome table with all possible syndromes (except 0) mapped to error patterns. Demonstrate looking up syndrome to find error location.
Draw circles representing valid codewords. Show how minimum distance is the closest distance between any two circles.

analogies.analogy: Like adding check digits in ISBN or credit card numbers - extra digits that help verify correctness.
Think of codewords as cities on a map. d_min is the shortest distance between any two cities - larger distance means easier to distinguish.
Like a tally mark that ensures total count is even - any odd change is noticed.
Like compression ratio - higher rate means more data, less overhead, but less error protection.
